food_subgroup: Thai set meal with grilled fish, hot and sour soup, and stir-fried vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a composite Thai BBQ seabass set meal. FOODON_03315091 (sea bass food product) is used as the closest parent anchor for the primary protein component.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (Sea bass) is used as the closest parent for the primary protein component. No composite Thai set meal code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for this composite Thai BBQ seabass set meal.

serving.description: 1 set meal (approximately 600 g total: ~250 g grilled seabass, ~200 g hot and sour chicken soup, ~150 g oyster sauce kailan)
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is a full Thai set meal comprising approximately 250 g of BBQ seabass, 200 g of hot and sour chicken soup, and 150 g of oyster-drizzled kailan, totalling approximately 600 g.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: Cooked sea bass (FDC ID 175159), Chicken soup (FDC ID 172940), Cooked Chinese broccoli / kailan with oyster sauce (analogous to cooked broccoli with sauce, FDC ID 169967); weighted composite per serving proportions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exists for this composite Thai BBQ seabass set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of three USDA FoodData Central entries corresponding to the three components: grilled sea bass (~42% of serving weight), hot and sour chicken soup (~33%), and oyster sauce kailan (~25%). Per-100g values are derived from the weighted composite divided by total serving weight. Values are cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Thai fish and vegetable dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed fish and vegetable meals; analogous estimate based on GI of boiled/grilled fish dishes and low-GI vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite Thai BBQ seabass set meal. GI is estimated at 38 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entries for grilled fish (GI ~0–10, protein-dominant, negligible glycemic contribution), hot and sour soup with chicken (GI ~35–40, low due to protein and acid content from lime/lemongrass), and stir-fried kailan with oyster sauce (GI ~30–40, low-GI vegetable). Weighted composite GI estimated at 38. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (38 × 32.4) / 100 ≈ 12.3, where carbohydrate per 600 g serving = 5.4 × 6 = 32.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soup base
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that this set meal contains approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 2,520 mg per full 600 g serving), primarily from fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soup seasoning, which may exceed the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal; requesting reduced-sauce preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content supporting glycemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: This set meal has an estimated low glycemic index of 38 and provides approximately 11.2 g protein per 100 g, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugary sauces or white rice, as the high protein and fibre content from kailan help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load from sauces and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from seabass
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While seabass provides beneficial unsaturated fatty acids including omega-3s that support cardiovascular health, the high sodium content from fish sauce and oyster sauce in this set meal poses a concern for individuals with cardiovascular disease, and portion control or sauce reduction is recommended to limit sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: BBQ Seabass Set is a Thai restaurant set meal comprising three components: a whole or portioned BBQ (charcoal or gas-grilled) seabass (pla kapong), a hot and sour chicken soup (similar to Tom Kha or Tom Yum style with lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, chilli, and mushrooms), and oyster-drizzled kailan (Chinese broccoli stir-fried or blanched with oyster sauce and garlic). The seabass is typically marinated with lemongrass, fish sauce, and herbs before grilling, resulting in a smoky, aromatic exterior with moist, flaky white flesh. The soup is tangy, spicy, and herbaceous, while the kailan provides a slightly bitter, crunchy contrast with a savoury oyster sauce glaze. This set is served in Thai restaurants in Singapore, including at The Upper House - Funan. Nutritionally, the meal is high in lean protein (~11 g per 100 g), moderate in sodium (~420 mg per 100 g), low in saturated fat (~0.9 g per 100 g), and has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 38, making it a relatively balanced Thai restaurant meal. The kailan component contributes dietary fibre, vitamin C, and calcium. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium from fish sauce and oyster sauce, which may be significant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, this set meal is a favourable choice due to its low GI, high protein, and moderate carbohydrate content. The set is priced at SGD 48.80 at The Upper House - Funan on field survey data.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source entry exists for this composite Thai BBQ seabass set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central component entries for grilled sea bass, chicken soup, and cooked kailan with oyster sauce. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. This is a composite set meal record estimated from three USDA FoodData Central component entries. No single approved source entry covers this exact dish. 2. Sodium is high (~420 mg/100 g, ~2,520 mg per full serving) due to fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soup seasoning; this is the primary clinical concern. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed as naturally zero for grilled fish and vegetable components with no hydrogenated fat ingredients. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this estimation. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this estimation. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported with sufficient confidence across all three component entries to produce a reliable composite estimate. 7. GI is estimated at 38 (low) using analogous entries from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite meal. 8. Glycemic load per serving = (38 × 32.4) / 100 ≈ 12.3, where carbohydrate per 600 g serving = 5.4 × 6 = 32.4 g. 9. Halal status is not confirmed as oyster sauce and fish sauce sourcing from the restaurant has not been verified. 10. This record was sourced from field survey data (The Upper House - Funan) at SGD 48.80 and processed under Project Veritas v2.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed weighted composite nutrient profile from three USDA FoodData Central component entries (grilled sea bass, chicken soup, cooked kailan with oyster sauce).
Added Thai local name (ชุดปลากะพงย่าง) and romanized form (Chut Pla Kapong Yang) to satisfy G6 Thai cuisine multilingual requirement.
Added Chinese simplified and traditional names for additional multilingual coverage.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit reasons in notes.
Documented trans_fat_g = 0.0 as naturally zero in notes.
Set GI to 38 (numeric, >0) with analogous_estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and full GL calculation documented.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent — all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in aliases.
